title: Listening - Part 4: Learning to Play an Instrument
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
topic: five people describing their experience of taking up a musical instrument in their fifties

content.audio_script: Speaker 1: I’d always been a huge fan of jazz, so [21]I started learning the saxophone because I wanted to understand the music I love from the inside out[/21]. I wanted to be a participant, not just a listener. [26]The most unexpected benefit has been the dramatic improvement in my lung capacity[/26]. My doctor was actually surprised by how much my respiratory health has improved. It’s given me more energy for other activities like hiking. || Speaker 2: I’m a very busy executive, and [22]I took up the cello because I needed a hobby that would force me to completely switch off from work[/22]. You can't think about spreadsheets while playing Bach. [27]What I find most difficult is the frustration of how long it takes to produce a decent sound[/27]. I’m used to mastering things quickly, so being a beginner again was very humbling. It’s taught me a lot about patience and perseverance. || Speaker 3: I recently retired and [23]I started the piano because I wanted to keep my mind active and prevent cognitive decline[/23]. I wanted to give my brain a challenge. [28]The most rewarding aspect has been the wonderful social life I’ve found at my music school[/28]. I’ve met a group of other late learners, and we’ve formed a small ensemble. We meet every week and then go for coffee. It’s been a great way to avoid the isolation of retirement. || Speaker 4: I’d always regretted giving up the violin as a child, so [24]I took it up again primarily to fulfill a promise I’d made to my younger self[/24]. I wanted to finish what I started. [29]The biggest hurdle has been the physical stiffness in my fingers[/29], which makes certain passages very painful to play. I’ve had to do a lot of exercises just to keep my joints flexible. It’s a physical battle as much as a musical one. || Speaker 5: I’m a high school teacher, and [25]I started learning the drums because I wanted to find a way to bond with my teenage son who plays the guitar[/25]. I wanted us to have a shared project. [30]The most significant outcome has been the massive boost in my ability to concentrate for long periods[/30]. Learning complex rhythms has trained my brain to stay present and focused, which has surprisingly helped me with my lesson planning and grading. I’m much more efficient now.

task1.title: Task One
task1.instruction: For questions 21–25, choose from the list (A–H) what primarily motivated each speaker to learn an instrument.

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: Speaker 1 wanted to understand jazz music from the inside out.

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: Speaker 2 needed a hobby to help her switch off from work stress.

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: Speaker 3 wanted to keep his mind active and prevent cognitive decline.

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: Speaker 4 wanted to fulfill a promise to her younger self and finish what she started.

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: Speaker 5 wanted to bond with his son through a shared musical project.

options.A: to prepare for a specific performance
options.C: to connect with a family member
options.D: to save money on professional lessons
options.H: to resolve a sense of past regret

task2.title: Task Two
task2.instruction: For questions 26–30, choose from the list (A–H) what each speaker mentions as an outcome of their experience.

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: Speaker 1 mentions a dramatic improvement in his lung capacity and respiratory health.

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: Speaker 2 found it humbling and frustrating to be a slow-learning beginner.

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: Speaker 3 highlights the social life and ensemble he found at his music school.

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: Speaker 4 mentions the physical stiffness and pain in her fingers.

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: Speaker 5 notes a massive boost in his ability to concentrate for long periods.

options.A: a change in their career aspirations
options.B: the discovery of a hidden talent
options.C: the pleasure of learning music theory
options.E: a surprising improvement in their focus
options.G: struggling with a lack of quick progress
options.H: the development of a new social group
